title: Intel 8085 I/O Write Timing Diagram (OUT Instruction)
examTip: The I/O Write cycle is similar to the Memory Write cycle, but IO/M̄ is HIGH (1). It consists of 3 T-states (T1-T3). In T1, the 8-bit I/O port address is sent on BOTH A8-A15 AND AD0-AD7 (duplicated), and ALE goes high. In T2, the CPU places write data on AD0-AD7, and WR̄ goes LOW. In T3, WR̄ goes HIGH, writing data into the peripheral. Status: S1=0, S0=1 (IO Write). IO/M̄ = 1 throughout.

blocks.label: I/O Port Address (Duplicated on upper bus)
I/O Port Address (8-bit)
blocks.note: Same port address on lower bus T1

blocks.label: WR̄ = 0 (LOW) — I/O Write Active
blocks.note: Peripheral latches data on rising edge

blocks.label: IO/M̄ = 1 (HIGH — I/O operation, not memory)
blocks.note: HIGH throughout — I/O write (not memory write)

blocks.label: S1 = 0, S0 = 1 (I/O Write Status)
blocks.note: S1=0 S0=1 uniquely identifies I/O Write cycle

blocks.label: Key Difference from Memory Write: IO/M̄ = 1 (HIGH) vs IO/M̄ = 0 (LOW). Port address 8-bit duplicated on A8-A15 and AD0-AD7. WR̄ rising edge in T3 writes data into peripheral.

theory:
The I/O Write machine cycle (executed by the 8085's OUT instruction) sends one byte of data from the Accumulator to a specified 8-bit I/O port. It is structurally identical to the Memory Write cycle except that IO/M̄ is HIGH (1) instead of LOW (0).

Step-by-Step Operation:

1. State T1:
- The 8-bit I/O port address is placed simultaneously on both A8-A15 and AD0-AD7.
- IO/M̄ is set HIGH to identify this as an I/O operation.
- Status bits S1=0, S0=1 identify this as an I/O Write operation.
- ALE pulses HIGH; falling edge latches the port address in the external decoder.

2. State T2:
- The CPU replaces the port address on AD0-AD7 with the write data byte (from the Accumulator).
- The CPU asserts WR̄ LOW, enabling the write-input of the addressed I/O device.
- The data byte remains stable on AD0-AD7 while WR̄ is LOW.

3. State T3:
- Write data continues to be held stable on AD0-AD7.
- WR̄ returns HIGH mid-T3. The rising edge causes the I/O peripheral to latch the data byte.
- The CPU de-asserts status signals and ends the machine cycle.
keyPoints: The 8085 I/O Write cycle (OUT instruction) consists of exactly 3 T-states: T1, T2, T3.
IO/M̄ = 1 (HIGH) throughout — key difference from Memory Write (IO/M̄ = 0).
The 8-bit I/O port address is duplicated: placed on BOTH A8-A15 AND AD0-AD7 in T1.
ALE pulses HIGH in T1; its falling edge latches the port address in the external decoder.
In T2, the CPU places write data (from Accumulator) on AD0-AD7 and asserts WR̄ LOW.
WR̄ goes HIGH mid-T3; the rising edge writes data into the peripheral. Status: S1=0, S0=1.
useCases: Analyzing bus timing when sending data to a peripheral connected to the 8085.
Understanding the execution of the OUT instruction in 8085 assembly language.
Distinguishing I/O Write from Memory Write using IO/M̄ and status signals.
